planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram URL Summarizer Bot

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that accepts any URL, extracts the main readable content, and returns a concise 3-sentence summary. For x.com and twitter.com links, it uses a dedicated extraction path to summarize a single tweet or a detected thread when available.

The bot runs as one Node.js ESM service using grammY, with TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN loaded from environment variables. MongoDB can be used for user preferences, caching, rate-limit state, and operational logs; simple in-memory maps are acceptable for a smaller MVP but should be treated as non-durable.

Product goals:
The bot should make link consumption fast inside Telegram. A user sends a URL, and the bot replies with a clear 3-sentence summary without requiring extra commands.

Core goals:

1) Accept URLs from normal Telegram messages, including messages that contain text plus one or more links.

2) Fetch and extract the meaningful content from general web pages, avoiding navigation menus, cookie banners, sidebars, ads, and unrelated boilerplate.

3) Detect x.com and twitter.com URLs and summarize the tweet or thread content using a specialized extractor rather than treating them like normal web pages.

4) Return exactly 3 useful sentences by default, written in a neutral tone and optimized for quick reading.

5) Provide /start instructions so users immediately understand how to use the bot.

6) Be safe and reliable in production: prevent server-side request forgery, enforce limits, handle slow pages, avoid duplicate work through caching, and give helpful error messages.

High-level architecture:
The service is a single Node.js ESM process running a grammY bot. It receives Telegram updates, classifies messages, extracts URLs, fetches page content, summarizes it, and replies in chat. The process should be structured as clear internal modules rather than a monolithic handler.

Recommended internal components:

1) Bot interface: grammY setup, command handlers, message handlers, error boundary, reply formatting, and Telegram-specific rate handling.

2) URL detector and normalizer: finds URLs in message text and Telegram entities, expands missing schemes when safe, normalizes hostnames, removes tracking parameters when appropriate, and creates a stable cache key.

3) Safety validator: blocks private IP ranges, localhost, unsupported schemes, suspicious redirects, oversized responses, and non-text content unless explicitly supported later.

4) Fetch service: performs HTTP requests with strict timeouts, redirect limits, size limits, content-type checks, user-agent identification, and retry rules only for safe transient failures.

5) Content extraction service: converts HTML into readable text, extracts title, author, publish date, canonical URL, and main body text. It should strip scripts, styles, navigation, comments, cookie prompts, and repeated boilerplate.

6) X/Twitter adapter: detects x.com and twitter.com URLs, extracts tweet identifiers, attempts to retrieve visible tweet or thread text, handles unavailable or protected content gracefully, and returns a normalized document similar to the generic extractor.

7) Summarization service: receives normalized document text and metadata, enforces token and character budgets, asks the summarizer for exactly 3 sentences, and performs output validation to keep responses concise.

8) Cache and persistence layer: stores completed summaries, normalized metadata, user settings, rate-limit counters, and operational events in MongoDB. For MVP deployments, in-memory maps can be used for cache and throttling, but this loses data on restart.

9) Observability and admin diagnostics: structured logs, request IDs per Telegram update, timing metrics for fetch and summarization phases, and summarized failure reasons without storing sensitive full message content by default.

The architecture should treat summarization as an abstraction. The bot should not hard-code business logic into a single model provider. A summarizer interface allows changing providers, prompts, cost controls, and fallback behavior without rewriting Telegram handlers.

Data model:
MongoDB is the recommended persistence option because it is already integrated and sufficient for this single-process bot. The schema can stay simple and document-oriented.

Suggested collections:

1) users: stores Telegram user ID, first seen time, last seen time, language preference, summary length preference if added later, consent or privacy preference if needed, and per-user counters.

2) urlsummaries: stores normalized URL, canonical URL, content hash, source type such as genericweb or xtwitter, title, extracted text hash, 3-sentence summary, detected language, creation time, last used time, and expiration time. This enables fast repeated summaries and reduces external fetch and summarization cost.

3) fetchevents: stores compact operational records such as URL host, status category, timing, error type, content length, redirect count, and timestamp. Avoid storing full page text unless there is a clear operational need.

4) ratelimits: stores counters by Telegram user ID, chat ID, and optionally host. For a single process, these may also be kept in memory, but MongoDB persistence makes limits survive restarts.

5) userfeedback: optional collection for storing simple feedback like useful or not useful, tied to a summary ID rather than raw private messages.

Important data rules:

1) Store the minimum content needed. Prefer storing summaries and hashes over full extracted text.

2) Apply retention windows for fetch logs and cached summaries.

3) Normalize URLs before caching so the same page is not summarized repeatedly due to tracking parameters.

4) Preserve enough metadata to debug issues without collecting unnecessary personal data.

5) Do not store Telegram tokens, summarizer secrets, or other credentials in MongoDB.

Key flows:
Start flow:

1) User sends /start.

2) Bot replies with short instructions: send any article, blog, documentation, news, or x.com/twitter.com link, and the bot will reply with a 3-sentence summary.

3) Bot mentions limitations: private pages, paywalled content, deleted tweets, protected tweets, and pages that block automated fetching may not work.

URL summary flow:

1) User sends a message containing one or more URLs.

2) Bot extracts URLs and selects the first valid URL for MVP. A later version can summarize multiple links sequentially with a per-message cap.

3) Bot checks user and chat rate limits. If the user is over limit, it replies with a friendly retry message.

4) Bot normalizes the URL and checks the cache. If a fresh cached summary exists, it replies immediately.

5) Bot validates the target to prevent unsafe requests, including private network addresses, unsupported protocols, excessive redirects, and oversized content.

6) Bot fetches the content with timeouts and size limits.

7) If the URL is x.com or twitter.com, the X/Twitter adapter attempts to extract the tweet or thread text. If the content is unavailable, protected, deleted, or blocked, the bot explains that it cannot access the tweet content.

8) For generic web pages, the extractor builds a readable document from the main page content.

9) The summarization service produces exactly 3 sentences. The bot validates that the answer is not empty, not too long, and not hallucinating unsupported claims when the source text is too short.

10) Bot replies with the summary, source title or hostname, and a short note if the summary was generated from partial content.

Failure flow:

1) If URL parsing fails, ask the user to send a valid link.

2) If the page is unreachable, reply that the page could not be fetched and suggest trying again later.

3) If the page has little readable text, reply that there was not enough content to summarize.

4) If summarization fails, optionally retry once for transient errors, then reply with a temporary failure message.

5) All failures should be logged with compact error categories for operational visibility.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework:

1) Node.js using ESM modules.

2) grammY for Telegram bot handling.

3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN loaded from environment variables.

4) Single Node.js service/process deployed on a generic Node.js server in the cloud.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB for users, cache, rate-limit state, summary metadata, and operational logs.

2) Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for an MVP or local development, especially for short-lived cache and rate limits, but they are not durable across restarts.

Networking and extraction:

1) Native HTTP fetch or a maintained HTTP client library with support for timeouts, redirect limits, headers, and response size controls.

2) HTML parsing and readability extraction libraries to isolate main article text from boilerplate.

3) Dedicated adapter for x.com and twitter.com URLs to extract tweet or thread text when publicly accessible.

Summarization:

1) A model-provider abstraction that accepts extracted source text and returns a 3-sentence summary.

2) Prompt templates and output validation owned by the application, not scattered across Telegram handlers.

Storage for files:

1) No file storage is required for the core version.

2) If future diagnostics require storing snapshots or images, use object storage or simple filesystem storage without making it part of the core dependency path.

Configuration:

1) Environment variables for Telegram token, MongoDB connection details, summarization credentials, timeout values, cache TTLs, and rate-limit settings.

2) Safe defaults for local development and stricter limits for production.

Non-functional requirements:
Security:

1) Enforce SSRF protections before every fetch, including blocking localhost, private IP ranges, link-local addresses, and unsupported schemes.

2) Re-check safety after redirects because a public URL can redirect to a private address.

3) Limit maximum response size, maximum extracted text length, redirect count, and total processing time per request.

4) Do not execute page JavaScript in the core design. Static fetching and parsing is safer and cheaper.

5) Avoid logging full user messages, full page contents, or secrets.

Reliability:

1) Use strict timeouts for fetch, extraction, and summarization.

2) Use graceful error messages rather than exposing stack traces.

3) Cache successful summaries to reduce latency, cost, and repeated external traffic.

4) Handle Telegram API errors centrally through grammY error handling.

5) Support graceful shutdown so in-flight requests can complete briefly and MongoDB connections close cleanly.

Performance:

1) Keep per-user concurrency limited so one user cannot monopolize the single process.

2) Use cache-first behavior for repeated links.

3) Truncate or chunk extracted text before summarization based on model limits while preserving title, headings, and early main content.

4) Return a typing or processing indication for slower pages so the user knows the bot is working.

Privacy and compliance:

1) Make /privacy available to explain what is stored and for how long.

2) Do not claim access to private or protected content.

3) Respect content availability constraints and provide clear messages for paywalled, blocked, or deleted pages.

4) Keep retention periods configurable for logs and cached summaries.

Operational readiness:

1) Structured logs with request IDs, Telegram update IDs, timing, and outcome category.

2) Health check endpoint or internal health function for process supervision on a generic Node.js server.

3) Configurable rate limits by user and chat.

4) Admin-visible aggregate metrics can be derived from MongoDB logs without adding extra infrastructure.

Bot commands:
• /start — Shows onboarding instructions and explains that users can send any URL to receive a 3-sentence summary. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows supported link types, common limitations, and examples of messages the bot understands. (usage: /help) [core]
• /privacy — Explains what data the bot stores, such as Telegram user ID, normalized URLs, cached summaries, and operational logs. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /settings — Optional command for future user preferences such as language preference or summary style. The initial production version can keep this minimal. (usage: /settings) [preferences]

DOCS.md.content:
# Link Brief Bot

Link Brief is a Telegram bot that summarizes URLs sent by users. Send a webpage, x.com, or twitter.com link and the bot replies with exactly 3 sentences when the content can be accessed publicly.

## Commands

### /start

Shows quick instructions. It tells users to send any URL and explains that public x.com and twitter.com links are supported.

Usage:

/start

### /help

Explains supported webpage links, X/Twitter tweet and thread links, and common failure cases.

Usage:

/help

## Message behavior

Send any normal Telegram text message containing one or more URLs. The bot detects up to 3 URLs, processes each one, fetches readable content, and summarizes each source in exactly 3 sentences.

If no URL is found, the bot asks the user to send a link.

In group chats, the bot only responds when mentioned by username or when the message replies to the bot.

## Supported links

1) Public webpages with readable HTML or text content.
2) Public x.com or twitter.com tweet and thread links when CookMyBots X Gateway is configured.

## Known limitations

1) Private pages cannot be summarized.
2) Login walls and paywalls may block extraction.
3) Deleted tweets, protected tweets, unavailable threads, or rate-limited X content cannot be summarized.
4) PDFs, videos, images, downloads, and other non-text content are rejected.
5) Some websites block automated fetches or return very little readable text.
6) The bot does not execute page JavaScript.

## Environment variables

TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is required. It is the Telegram bot token from BotFather.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT is required. It must be the CookMyBots AI Gateway base URL, not a /chat route.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY is required. It authenticates requests to the CookMyBots AI Gateway.

COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT is optional. It enables X/Twitter content retrieval through the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy.

COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY is optional. It authenticates requests to the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy. The user must also connect an X account in CookMyBots.

AI_TIMEOUT_MS is optional and defaults to 600000.

AI_MAX_RETRIES is optional and defaults to 2.

CONCURRENCY is optional and defaults to 20, but the bot caps AI summarization work conservatively to protect memory.

## Setup

1) Install dependencies with npm install.
2) Copy .env.sample to .env.
3) Fill TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT, and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY.
4) Optionally fill COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY for X/Twitter links.
5) Run npm run dev locally or npm start in production.

## Deployment notes

The bot runs as one Node.js process. It uses grammY and long polling through @grammyjs/runner. On startup, it clears any Telegram webhook with drop_pending_updates to avoid polling conflicts.

## Troubleshooting

If the bot exits immediately, check that TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT, and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY are set.

If X/Twitter links return a configuration message, set COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY and connect an X account in CookMyBots.

If a webpage cannot be summarized, it may be blocked, private, too large, unsupported, or missing readable text.

Logs print safe diagnostics only. They show whether environment variables are present but never print token or API key values.

README.md.content:
# Link Brief Bot

Link Brief is a Telegram URL summarizer built with Node.js ES modules and grammY. Users send links, and the bot replies with exactly 3 sentences.

## Features

1) Summarizes public webpage URLs.
2) Extracts readable HTML or text content and rejects unsupported content types.
3) Supports public x.com and twitter.com tweet or thread links through the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy when configured.
4) Uses the CookMyBots AI Gateway for summarization.
5) Includes /start and /help commands.
6) Runs as a single Node.js process with long polling.
7) Adds safe logs for startup, URL detection, fetches, X gateway calls, AI calls, polling, and runtime errors.

## Architecture

src/index.js starts the bot, validates required environment variables, clears Telegram webhooks, and starts long polling with @grammyjs/runner.

src/bot.js creates the grammY bot instance.

src/commands contains /start and /help.

src/features/urlSummarizer.js detects URLs, applies group reply rules, enforces backpressure, and routes links to the right extractor.

src/services/webPage.js safely fetches and extracts readable webpage text.

src/services/xGateway.js retrieves public tweet or thread content through the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy.

src/services/summarizer.js calls the AI helper and enforces exactly 3 sentences.

src/lib/ai.js wraps CookMyBots AI Gateway /chat requests.

No database is used because this bot does not need long-term memory.

## Setup

1) Install Node.js 18 or newer.
2) Run npm install.
3) Copy .env.sample to .env.
4) Fill the required values.
5) Run npm run dev for local development.
6) Run npm start in production.

## Environment variables

TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is required.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT is required and must be the base URL, for example https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY is required.

COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT is optional for X/Twitter support.

COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY is optional for X/Twitter support.

AI_TIMEOUT_MS is optional and defaults to 600000.

AI_MAX_RETRIES is optional and defaults to 2.

CONCURRENCY is optional and defaults to 20.

## Commands

/start replies with onboarding instructions.

/help explains supported links and common failure cases.

## Examples

User sends:

https://example.com/article

Bot replies with a 3-sentence summary.

User sends:

https://x.com/someone/status/1234567890

Bot retrieves public tweet or thread text through CookMyBots X Gateway if configured, then replies with a 3-sentence summary.

## External integrations

Telegram Bot API is accessed through grammY.

CookMyBots AI Gateway is called at POST {COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT}/chat with Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY.

CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy is called at POST {COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT}/proxy with Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY. The bot never calls api.x.com or api.twitter.com directly.

## Deployment

Deploy as one Node.js web service or worker-style service that runs npm start. Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT, and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY in the deployment environment. Add COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY only if X/Twitter summarization is needed.

## Troubleshooting

If startup fails, check required env vars. Startup logs only print true or false for secret presence.

If Telegram polling conflicts happen during deploy overlap, the runner backs off and retries.

If a URL fails, the page may be private, blocked, too large, deleted, behind a login wall, rate-limited, or not text-based.

If X/Twitter fails, verify the X Gateway env vars and connect an X account in CookMyBots.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram URL Summarizer Bot

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that accepts any URL, extracts the main readable content, and returns a concise 3-sentence summary. For x.com and twitter.com links, it uses a dedicated extraction path to summarize a single tweet or a detected thread when available.

The bot runs as one Node.js ESM service using grammY, with TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN loaded from environment variables. MongoDB can be used for user preferences, caching, rate-limit state, and operational logs; simple in-memory maps are acceptable for a smaller MVP but should be treated as non-durable.

Product goals:
The bot should make link consumption fast inside Telegram. A user sends a URL, and the bot replies with a clear 3-sentence summary without requiring extra commands.

Core goals:

1) Accept URLs from normal Telegram messages, including messages that contain text plus one or more links.

2) Fetch and extract the meaningful content from general web pages, avoiding navigation menus, cookie banners, sidebars, ads, and unrelated boilerplate.

3) Detect x.com and twitter.com URLs and summarize the tweet or thread content using a specialized extractor rather than treating them like normal web pages.

4) Return exactly 3 useful sentences by default, written in a neutral tone and optimized for quick reading.

5) Provide /start instructions so users immediately understand how to use the bot.

6) Be safe and reliable in production: prevent server-side request forgery, enforce limits, handle slow pages, avoid duplicate work through caching, and give helpful error messages.

High-level architecture:
The service is a single Node.js ESM process running a grammY bot. It receives Telegram updates, classifies messages, extracts URLs, fetches page content, summarizes it, and replies in chat. The process should be structured as clear internal modules rather than a monolithic handler.

Recommended internal components:

1) Bot interface: grammY setup, command handlers, message handlers, error boundary, reply formatting, and Telegram-specific rate handling.

2) URL detector and normalizer: finds URLs in message text and Telegram entities, expands missing schemes when safe, normalizes hostnames, removes tracking parameters when appropriate, and creates a stable cache key.

3) Safety validator: blocks private IP ranges, localhost, unsupported schemes, suspicious redirects, oversized responses, and non-text content unless explicitly supported later.

4) Fetch service: performs HTTP requests with strict timeouts, redirect limits, size limits, content-type checks, user-agent identification, and retry rules only for safe transient failures.

5) Content extraction service: converts HTML into readable text, extracts title, author, publish date, canonical URL, and main body text. It should strip scripts, styles, navigation, comments, cookie prompts, and repeated boilerplate.

6) X/Twitter adapter: detects x.com and twitter.com URLs, extracts tweet identifiers, attempts to retrieve visible tweet or thread text, handles unavailable or protected content gracefully, and returns a normalized document similar to the generic extractor.

7) Summarization service: receives normalized document text and metadata, enforces token and character budgets, asks the summarizer for exactly 3 sentences, and performs output validation to keep responses concise.

8) Cache and persistence layer: stores completed summaries, normalized metadata, user settings, rate-limit counters, and operational events in MongoDB. For MVP deployments, in-memory maps can be used for cache and throttling, but this loses data on restart.

9) Observability and admin diagnostics: structured logs, request IDs per Telegram update, timing metrics for fetch and summarization phases, and summarized failure reasons without storing sensitive full message content by default.

The architecture should treat summarization as an abstraction. The bot should not hard-code business logic into a single model provider. A summarizer interface allows changing providers, prompts, cost controls, and fallback behavior without rewriting Telegram handlers.

Data model:
MongoDB is the recommended persistence option because it is already integrated and sufficient for this single-process bot. The schema can stay simple and document-oriented.

Suggested collections:

1) users: stores Telegram user ID, first seen time, last seen time, language preference, summary length preference if added later, consent or privacy preference if needed, and per-user counters.

2) urlsummaries: stores normalized URL, canonical URL, content hash, source type such as genericweb or xtwitter, title, extracted text hash, 3-sentence summary, detected language, creation time, last used time, and expiration time. This enables fast repeated summaries and reduces external fetch and summarization cost.

3) fetchevents: stores compact operational records such as URL host, status category, timing, error type, content length, redirect count, and timestamp. Avoid storing full page text unless there is a clear operational need.

4) ratelimits: stores counters by Telegram user ID, chat ID, and optionally host. For a single process, these may also be kept in memory, but MongoDB persistence makes limits survive restarts.

5) userfeedback: optional collection for storing simple feedback like useful or not useful, tied to a summary ID rather than raw private messages.

Important data rules:

1) Store the minimum content needed. Prefer storing summaries and hashes over full extracted text.

2) Apply retention windows for fetch logs and cached summaries.

3) Normalize URLs before caching so the same page is not summarized repeatedly due to tracking parameters.

4) Preserve enough metadata to debug issues without collecting unnecessary personal data.

5) Do not store Telegram tokens, summarizer secrets, or other credentials in MongoDB.

Key flows:
Start flow:

1) User sends /start.

2) Bot replies with short instructions: send any article, blog, documentation, news, or x.com/twitter.com link, and the bot will reply with a 3-sentence summary.

3) Bot mentions limitations: private pages, paywalled content, deleted tweets, protected tweets, and pages that block automated fetching may not work.

URL summary flow:

1) User sends a message containing one or more URLs.

2) Bot extracts URLs and selects the first valid URL for MVP. A later version can summarize multiple links sequentially with a per-message cap.

3) Bot checks user and chat rate limits. If the user is over limit, it replies with a friendly retry message.

4) Bot normalizes the URL and checks the cache. If a fresh cached summary exists, it replies immediately.

5) Bot validates the target to prevent unsafe requests, including private network addresses, unsupported protocols, excessive redirects, and oversized content.

6) Bot fetches the content with timeouts and size limits.

7) If the URL is x.com or twitter.com, the X/Twitter adapter attempts to extract the tweet or thread text. If the content is unavailable, protected, deleted, or blocked, the bot explains that it cannot access the tweet content.

8) For generic web pages, the extractor builds a readable document from the main page content.

9) The summarization service produces exactly 3 sentences. The bot validates that the answer is not empty, not too long, and not hallucinating unsupported claims when the source text is too short.

10) Bot replies with the summary, source title or hostname, and a short note if the summary was generated from partial content.

Failure flow:

1) If URL parsing fails, ask the user to send a valid link.

2) If the page is unreachable, reply that the page could not be fetched and suggest trying again later.

3) If the page has little readable text, reply that there was not enough content to summarize.

4) If summarization fails, optionally retry once for transient errors, then reply with a temporary failure message.

5) All failures should be logged with compact error categories for operational visibility.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework:

1) Node.js using ESM modules.

2) grammY for Telegram bot handling.

3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN loaded from environment variables.

4) Single Node.js service/process deployed on a generic Node.js server in the cloud.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB for users, cache, rate-limit state, summary metadata, and operational logs.

2) Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for an MVP or local development, especially for short-lived cache and rate limits, but they are not durable across restarts.

Networking and extraction:

1) Native HTTP fetch or a maintained HTTP client library with support for timeouts, redirect limits, headers, and response size controls.

2) HTML parsing and readability extraction libraries to isolate main article text from boilerplate.

3) Dedicated adapter for x.com and twitter.com URLs to extract tweet or thread text when publicly accessible.

Summarization:

1) A model-provider abstraction that accepts extracted source text and returns a 3-sentence summary.

2) Prompt templates and output validation owned by the application, not scattered across Telegram handlers.

Storage for files:

1) No file storage is required for the core version.

2) If future diagnostics require storing snapshots or images, use object storage or simple filesystem storage without making it part of the core dependency path.

Configuration:

1) Environment variables for Telegram token, MongoDB connection details, summarization credentials, timeout values, cache TTLs, and rate-limit settings.

2) Safe defaults for local development and stricter limits for production.

Non-functional requirements:
Security:

1) Enforce SSRF protections before every fetch, including blocking localhost, private IP ranges, link-local addresses, and unsupported schemes.

2) Re-check safety after redirects because a public URL can redirect to a private address.

3) Limit maximum response size, maximum extracted text length, redirect count, and total processing time per request.

4) Do not execute page JavaScript in the core design. Static fetching and parsing is safer and cheaper.

5) Avoid logging full user messages, full page contents, or secrets.

Reliability:

1) Use strict timeouts for fetch, extraction, and summarization.

2) Use graceful error messages rather than exposing stack traces.

3) Cache successful summaries to reduce latency, cost, and repeated external traffic.

4) Handle Telegram API errors centrally through grammY error handling.

5) Support graceful shutdown so in-flight requests can complete briefly and MongoDB connections close cleanly.

Performance:

1) Keep per-user concurrency limited so one user cannot monopolize the single process.

2) Use cache-first behavior for repeated links.

3) Truncate or chunk extracted text before summarization based on model limits while preserving title, headings, and early main content.

4) Return a typing or processing indication for slower pages so the user knows the bot is working.

Privacy and compliance:

1) Make /privacy available to explain what is stored and for how long.

2) Do not claim access to private or protected content.

3) Respect content availability constraints and provide clear messages for paywalled, blocked, or deleted pages.

4) Keep retention periods configurable for logs and cached summaries.

Operational readiness:

1) Structured logs with request IDs, Telegram update IDs, timing, and outcome category.

2) Health check endpoint or internal health function for process supervision on a generic Node.js server.

3) Configurable rate limits by user and chat.

4) Admin-visible aggregate metrics can be derived from MongoDB logs without adding extra infrastructure.

Bot commands:
• /start — Shows onboarding instructions and explains that users can send any URL to receive a 3-sentence summary. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows supported link types, common limitations, and examples of messages the bot understands. (usage: /help) [core]
• /privacy — Explains what data the bot stores, such as Telegram user ID, normalized URLs, cached summaries, and operational logs. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /settings — Optional command for future user preferences such as language preference or summary style. The initial production version can keep this minimal. (usage: /settings) [preferences]

DOCS.md.content:
# Link Brief Bot

Link Brief is a Telegram bot that summarizes URLs sent by users. Send a webpage, x.com, or twitter.com link and the bot replies with exactly 3 sentences when the content can be accessed publicly.

## Commands

### /start

Shows quick instructions. It tells users to send any URL and explains that public x.com and twitter.com links are supported.

Usage:

/start

### /help

Explains supported webpage links, X/Twitter tweet and thread links, and common failure cases.

Usage:

/help

## Message behavior

Send any normal Telegram text message containing one or more URLs. The bot detects up to 3 URLs, processes each one, fetches readable content, and summarizes each source in exactly 3 sentences.

If no URL is found, the bot asks the user to send a link.

In group chats, the bot only responds when mentioned by username or when the message replies to the bot.

## Supported links

1) Public webpages with readable HTML or text content.
2) Public x.com or twitter.com tweet and thread links when CookMyBots X Gateway is configured.

## Known limitations

1) Private pages cannot be summarized.
2) Login walls and paywalls may block extraction.
3) Deleted tweets, protected tweets, unavailable threads, or rate-limited X content cannot be summarized.
4) PDFs, videos, images, downloads, and other non-text content are rejected.
5) Some websites block automated fetches or return very little readable text.
6) The bot does not execute page JavaScript.

## Environment variables

TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is required. It is the Telegram bot token from BotFather.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT is required. It must be the CookMyBots AI Gateway base URL, not a /chat route.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY is required. It authenticates requests to the CookMyBots AI Gateway.

COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT is optional. It enables X/Twitter content retrieval through the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy.

COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY is optional. It authenticates requests to the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy. The user must also connect an X account in CookMyBots.

AI_TIMEOUT_MS is optional and defaults to 600000.

AI_MAX_RETRIES is optional and defaults to 2.

CONCURRENCY is optional and defaults to 20, but the bot caps AI summarization work conservatively to protect memory.

## Setup

1) Install dependencies with npm install.
2) Copy .env.sample to .env.
3) Fill TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT, and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY.
4) Optionally fill COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY for X/Twitter links.
5) Run npm run dev locally or npm start in production.

## Deployment notes

The bot runs as one Node.js process. It uses grammY and long polling through @grammyjs/runner. On startup, it clears any Telegram webhook with drop_pending_updates to avoid polling conflicts.

## Troubleshooting

If the bot exits immediately, check that TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT, and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY are set.

If X/Twitter links return a configuration message, set COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY and connect an X account in CookMyBots.

If a webpage cannot be summarized, it may be blocked, private, too large, unsupported, or missing readable text.

Logs print safe diagnostics only. They show whether environment variables are present but never print token or API key values.

README.md.content:
# Link Brief Bot

Link Brief is a Telegram URL summarizer built with Node.js ES modules and grammY. Users send links, and the bot replies with exactly 3 sentences.

## Features

1) Summarizes public webpage URLs.
2) Extracts readable HTML or text content and rejects unsupported content types.
3) Supports public x.com and twitter.com tweet or thread links through the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy when configured.
4) Uses the CookMyBots AI Gateway for summarization.
5) Includes /start and /help commands.
6) Runs as a single Node.js process with long polling.
7) Adds safe logs for startup, URL detection, fetches, X gateway calls, AI calls, polling, and runtime errors.

## Architecture

src/index.js starts the bot, validates required environment variables, clears Telegram webhooks, and starts long polling with @grammyjs/runner.

src/bot.js creates the grammY bot instance.

src/commands contains /start and /help.

src/features/urlSummarizer.js detects URLs, applies group reply rules, enforces backpressure, and routes links to the right extractor.

src/services/webPage.js safely fetches and extracts readable webpage text.

src/services/xGateway.js retrieves public tweet or thread content through the CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy.

src/services/summarizer.js calls the AI helper and enforces exactly 3 sentences.

src/lib/ai.js wraps CookMyBots AI Gateway /chat requests.

No database is used because this bot does not need long-term memory.

## Setup

1) Install Node.js 18 or newer.
2) Run npm install.
3) Copy .env.sample to .env.
4) Fill the required values.
5) Run npm run dev for local development.
6) Run npm start in production.

## Environment variables

TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is required.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT is required and must be the base URL, for example https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY is required.

COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT is optional for X/Twitter support.

COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY is optional for X/Twitter support.

AI_TIMEOUT_MS is optional and defaults to 600000.

AI_MAX_RETRIES is optional and defaults to 2.

CONCURRENCY is optional and defaults to 20.

## Commands

/start replies with onboarding instructions.

/help explains supported links and common failure cases.

## Examples

User sends:

https://example.com/article

Bot replies with a 3-sentence summary.

User sends:

https://x.com/someone/status/1234567890

Bot retrieves public tweet or thread text through CookMyBots X Gateway if configured, then replies with a 3-sentence summary.

## External integrations

Telegram Bot API is accessed through grammY.

CookMyBots AI Gateway is called at POST {COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT}/chat with Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY.

CookMyBots X Gateway Proxy is called at POST {COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT}/proxy with Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY. The bot never calls api.x.com or api.twitter.com directly.

## Deployment

Deploy as one Node.js web service or worker-style service that runs npm start. Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT, and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY in the deployment environment. Add COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY only if X/Twitter summarization is needed.

## Troubleshooting

If startup fails, check required env vars. Startup logs only print true or false for secret presence.

If Telegram polling conflicts happen during deploy overlap, the runner backs off and retries.

If a URL fails, the page may be private, blocked, too large, deleted, behind a login wall, rate-limited, or not text-based.

If X/Twitter fails, verify the X Gateway env vars and connect an X account in CookMyBots.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram URL Summarizer Bot

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that accepts any URL, extracts the main readable content, and returns a concise 3-sentence summary. For x.com and twitter.com links, it uses a dedicated extraction path to summarize a single tweet or a detected thread when available.

The bot runs as one Node.js ESM service using grammY, with TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN loaded from environment variables. MongoDB can be used for user preferences, caching, rate-limit state, and operational logs; simple in-memory maps are acceptable for a smaller MVP but should be treated as non-durable.

Product goals:
The bot should make link consumption fast inside Telegram. A user sends a URL, and the bot replies with a clear 3-sentence summary without requiring extra commands.

Core goals:

1) Accept URLs from normal Telegram messages, including messages that contain text plus one or more links.

2) Fetch and extract the meaningful content from general web pages, avoiding navigation menus, cookie banners, sidebars, ads, and unrelated boilerplate.

3) Detect x.com and twitter.com URLs and summarize the tweet or thread content using a specialized extractor rather than treating them like normal web pages.

4) Return exactly 3 useful sentences by default, written in a neutral tone and optimized for quick reading.

5) Provide /start instructions so users immediately understand how to use the bot.

6) Be safe and reliable in production: prevent server-side request forgery, enforce limits, handle slow pages, avoid duplicate work through caching, and give helpful error messages.

High-level architecture:
The service is a single Node.js ESM process running a grammY bot. It receives Telegram updates, classifies messages, extracts URLs, fetches page content, summarizes it, and replies in chat. The process should be structured as clear internal modules rather than a monolithic handler.

Recommended internal components:

1) Bot interface: grammY setup, command handlers, message handlers, error boundary, reply formatting, and Telegram-specific rate handling.

2) URL detector and normalizer: finds URLs in message text and Telegram entities, expands missing schemes when safe, normalizes hostnames, removes tracking parameters when appropriate, and creates a stable cache key.

3) Safety validator: blocks private IP ranges, localhost, unsupported schemes, suspicious redirects, oversized responses, and non-text content unless explicitly supported later.

4) Fetch service: performs HTTP requests with strict timeouts, redirect limits, size limits, content-type checks, user-agent identification, and retry rules only for safe transient failures.

5) Content extraction service: converts HTML into readable text, extracts title, author, publish date, canonical URL, and main body text. It should strip scripts, styles, navigation, comments, cookie prompts, and repeated boilerplate.

6) X/Twitter adapter: detects x.com and twitter.com URLs, extracts tweet identifiers, attempts to retrieve visible tweet or thread text, handles unavailable or protected content gracefully, and returns a normalized document similar to the generic extractor.

7) Summarization service: receives normalized document text and metadata, enforces token and character budgets, asks the summarizer for exactly 3 sentences, and performs output validation to keep responses concise.

8) Cache and persistence layer: stores completed summaries, normalized metadata, user settings, rate-limit counters, and operational events in MongoDB. For MVP deployments, in-memory maps can be used for cache and throttling, but this loses data on restart.

9) Observability and admin diagnostics: structured logs, request IDs per Telegram update, timing metrics for fetch and summarization phases, and summarized failure reasons without storing sensitive full message content by default.

The architecture should treat summarization as an abstraction. The bot should not hard-code business logic into a single model provider. A summarizer interface allows changing providers, prompts, cost controls, and fallback behavior without rewriting Telegram handlers.

Data model:
MongoDB is the recommended persistence option because it is already integrated and sufficient for this single-process bot. The schema can stay simple and document-oriented.

Suggested collections:

1) users: stores Telegram user ID, first seen time, last seen time, language preference, summary length preference if added later, consent or privacy preference if needed, and per-user counters.

2) urlsummaries: stores normalized URL, canonical URL, content hash, source type such as genericweb or xtwitter, title, extracted text hash, 3-sentence summary, detected language, creation time, last used time, and expiration time. This enables fast repeated summaries and reduces external fetch and summarization cost.

3) fetchevents: stores compact operational records such as URL host, status category, timing, error type, content length, redirect count, and timestamp. Avoid storing full page text unless there is a clear operational need.

4) ratelimits: stores counters by Telegram user ID, chat ID, and optionally host. For a single process, these may also be kept in memory, but MongoDB persistence makes limits survive restarts.

5) userfeedback: optional collection for storing simple feedback like useful or not useful, tied to a summary ID rather than raw private messages.

Important data rules:

1) Store the minimum content needed. Prefer storing summaries and hashes over full extracted text.

2) Apply retention windows for fetch logs and cached summaries.

3) Normalize URLs before caching so the same page is not summarized repeatedly due to tracking parameters.

4) Preserve enough metadata to debug issues without collecting unnecessary personal data.

5) Do not store Telegram tokens, summarizer secrets, or other credentials in MongoDB.

Key flows:
Start flow:

1) User sends /start.

2) Bot replies with short instructions: send any article, blog, documentation, news, or x.com/twitter.com link, and the bot will reply with a 3-sentence summary.

3) Bot mentions limitations: private pages, paywalled content, deleted tweets, protected tweets, and pages that block automated fetching may not work.

URL summary flow:

1) User sends a message containing one or more URLs.

2) Bot extracts URLs and selects the first valid URL for MVP. A later version can summarize multiple links sequentially with a per-message cap.

3) Bot checks user and chat rate limits. If the user is over limit, it replies with a friendly retry message.

4) Bot normalizes the URL and checks the cache. If a fresh cached summary exists, it replies immediately.

5) Bot validates the target to prevent unsafe requests, including private network addresses, unsupported protocols, excessive redirects, and oversized content.

6) Bot fetches the content with timeouts and size limits.

7) If the URL is x.com or twitter.com, the X/Twitter adapter attempts to extract the tweet or thread text. If the content is unavailable, protected, deleted, or blocked, the bot explains that it cannot access the tweet content.

8) For generic web pages, the extractor builds a readable document from the main page content.

9) The summarization service produces exactly 3 sentences. The bot validates that the answer is not empty, not too long, and not hallucinating unsupported claims when the source text is too short.

10) Bot replies with the summary, source title or hostname, and a short note if the summary was generated from partial content.

Failure flow:

1) If URL parsing fails, ask the user to send a valid link.

2) If the page is unreachable, reply that the page could not be fetched and suggest trying again later.

3) If the page has little readable text, reply that there was not enough content to summarize.

4) If summarization fails, optionally retry once for transient errors, then reply with a temporary failure message.

5) All failures should be logged with compact error categories for operational visibility.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework:

1) Node.js using ESM modules.

2) grammY for Telegram bot handling.

3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN loaded from environment variables.

4) Single Node.js service/process deployed on a generic Node.js server in the cloud.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB for users, cache, rate-limit state, summary metadata, and operational logs.

2) Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for an MVP or local development, especially for short-lived cache and rate limits, but they are not durable across restarts.

Networking and extraction:

1) Native HTTP fetch or a maintained HTTP client library with support for timeouts, redirect limits, headers, and response size controls.

2) HTML parsing and readability extraction libraries to isolate main article text from boilerplate.

3) Dedicated adapter for x.com and twitter.com URLs to extract tweet or thread text when publicly accessible.

Summarization:

1) A model-provider abstraction that accepts extracted source text and returns a 3-sentence summary.

2) Prompt templates and output validation owned by the application, not scattered across Telegram handlers.

Storage for files:

1) No file storage is required for the core version.

2) If future diagnostics require storing snapshots or images, use object storage or simple filesystem storage without making it part of the core dependency path.

Configuration:

1) Environment variables for Telegram token, MongoDB connection details, summarization credentials, timeout values, cache TTLs, and rate-limit settings.

2) Safe defaults for local development and stricter limits for production.

Non-functional requirements:
Security:

1) Enforce SSRF protections before every fetch, including blocking localhost, private IP ranges, link-local addresses, and unsupported schemes.

2) Re-check safety after redirects because a public URL can redirect to a private address.

3) Limit maximum response size, maximum extracted text length, redirect count, and total processing time per request.

4) Do not execute page JavaScript in the core design. Static fetching and parsing is safer and cheaper.

5) Avoid logging full user messages, full page contents, or secrets.

Reliability:

1) Use strict timeouts for fetch, extraction, and summarization.

2) Use graceful error messages rather than exposing stack traces.

3) Cache successful summaries to reduce latency, cost, and repeated external traffic.

4) Handle Telegram API errors centrally through grammY error handling.

5) Support graceful shutdown so in-flight requests can complete briefly and MongoDB connections close cleanly.

Performance:

1) Keep per-user concurrency limited so one user cannot monopolize the single process.

2) Use cache-first behavior for repeated links.

3) Truncate or chunk extracted text before summarization based on model limits while preserving title, headings, and early main content.

4) Return a typing or processing indication for slower pages so the user knows the bot is working.

Privacy and compliance:

1) Make /privacy available to explain what is stored and for how long.

2) Do not claim access to private or protected content.

3) Respect content availability constraints and provide clear messages for paywalled, blocked, or deleted pages.

4) Keep retention periods configurable for logs and cached summaries.

Operational readiness:

1) Structured logs with request IDs, Telegram update IDs, timing, and outcome category.

2) Health check endpoint or internal health function for process supervision on a generic Node.js server.

3) Configurable rate limits by user and chat.

4) Admin-visible aggregate metrics can be derived from MongoDB logs without adding extra infrastructure.

Bot commands:
• /start — Shows onboarding instructions and explains that users can send any URL to receive a 3-sentence summary. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows supported link types, common limitations, and examples of messages the bot understands. (usage: /help) [core]
• /privacy — Explains what data the bot stores, such as Telegram user ID, normalized URLs, cached summaries, and operational logs. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /settings — Optional command for future user preferences such as language preference or summary style. The initial production version can keep this minimal. (usage: /settings) [preferences]
